name: Pocket Patch
tagline: Patch leaks before your money slips away.
description: Pocket Patch is a proactive money-management app that detects the small, recurring financial leaks in your life and turns them into actionable recovery plans. Instead of just categorizing spending after the fact, it scans subscriptions, repeat delivery orders, bank fees, forgotten free trials, and lifestyle habits that quietly drain cash, then suggests specific ways to patch each leak. It helps users in daily life by sending timely nudges like "You ordered coffee three times this week at the same place" or "Your streaming bundle overlaps with two services you never open," paired with one-tap options to cancel, downgrade, negotiate, or replace. A college graduate, for example, might discover that three forgotten trials are converting next week, while a parent could learn that school lunch app top-ups are causing constant tiny overdrafts. What makes it unique is its focus on behavior-based leak detection rather than generic budgeting, combining transaction patterns, calendar context, and merchant habits to predict where money is escaping before the month ends. It also creates a personalized recovery score that shows how much cash could be reclaimed in the next 30 days, making savings feel immediate and tangible. Over time, the app becomes a personal financial maintenance tool that helps users protect cash with minimal effort and less guilt.
features: The app identifies recurring money leaks, such as unused subscriptions, duplicated services, and hidden fees, so users can recover money they did not realize they were losing.
It predicts future drains by analyzing spending patterns and calendars, which helps users spot problems before charges hit their account.
One-tap patch actions let users cancel, downgrade, freeze, or negotiate recurring expenses without navigating multiple websites or support chats.
The app groups tiny, frequent purchases into behavior clusters, making it easier to understand habits like weekday coffee runs or late-night delivery spending that add up over time.
A recovery dashboard estimates how much money users could save in the next month if they applied the recommended fixes, which turns vague advice into concrete motivation.
audience: Young adults and busy households aged 22-45 who want better control over cash flow but do not have the time or patience for traditional budgeting. It is especially useful for people with multiple subscriptions, irregular income, or small habitual purchases that quietly erode savings.
useCases: Maya just got paid and wants to know why her checking account feels low even though she has not made any big purchases. She opens Pocket Patch and discovers two overlapping music services, a food delivery pass she forgot to cancel, and a string of small convenience-store runs that are costing her more than she thought.

Jordan is moving into a new apartment and wants to avoid the financial chaos of setting up his life all over again. He uses the app to map recurring expenses from his old place, cancel duplicated utilities add-ons, and set reminders for trial subscriptions before they convert.

Elena is a freelance designer with uneven income and needs to protect her cash during slower months. She opens Pocket Patch after a busy week and sees a forecast that her app-based grocery deliveries and ride-share subscriptions could create a cash squeeze next week, so she downgrades them before it becomes a problem.
